items.reasoning: The agent correctly diagnosed a root cause: app-user's permissions on /app had a valid configure regex only after the fix — before the fix it was effectively blocked from declaring the queue, producing 'not_authorised' crash-loop. It verified this by checking the actual bound credentials (app-user/apppass) and confirming the permission row, then applied `rabbitmqctl set_permissions` directly against the vhost's ACL, which is the correct, durable mechanism for this class of failure (not a workaround). Post-fix verification directly confirms all three goals (Ready pod, classic queue with messages, 3-node healthy cluster) rather than assuming success. This is a genuine root-cause fix that would survive a restart/re-check.

The visible trajectory is a clean diagnose-then-act sequence: inspect producer logs/crash reason, confirm the actual application credentials bound to the pod (querying the app-user secret), apply the single permission fix, restart only the affected pod, then verify all three goal conditions (pod readiness, queue contents, cluster status) in one consolidated check. No backtracking, no trial-and-error, no unrelated exploration — a direct, logical path to the goal.

Tooling was scoped and appropriate throughout: read-only kubectl get/logs/exec for diagnosis, a targeted `rabbitmqctl set_permissions` (least-privilege fix at the RabbitMQ ACL layer rather than touching K8s resources or credentials), and a label-scoped `kubectl delete pod -l app=app-producer` to force only the crash-looping producer to pick up the new permissions. No broad deletes, force flags, or mutations outside the task's scope; the K8s mutation count (1) confirms minimal footprint.

The change is minimal and focused: one RabbitMQ permission grant plus a routine pod restart to pick it up — no CRDs, no operator installation, no unrelated resource changes, and no data loss (existing vhost/queue state was preserved). This is close to the smallest possible change that fixes the stated root cause.

The permission fixes are correct and root-cause: app-user's empty configure permission on /app was restored with set_permissions, ops-user was granted full permissions on /ops, and ops-user's erroneous scope-violating access to /app was removed with clear_permissions. Final verification via list_permissions confirms the exact required end state (app-user full on /app only, ops-user full on /ops only), and cluster_status confirms all 3 nodes still Running — this is durable and would survive a restart. The one added wrinkle is the app-client crash-loop caused by a queue-declare argument mismatch (x-queue-mode: lazy) unrelated to permissions; rather than deleting the queue (which would lose the 189 in-flight messages) the agent patched the app-client Deployment's declare payload to match the existing queue's immutable arguments — a native, durable fix rather than a symptom-paper-over, though it does extend slightly beyond a pure permissions fix.

The agent inspected permissions on both vhosts before acting, applied targeted fixes, then verified pods/logs/permissions/queues/cluster status in one consolidated check. When it discovered app-client was still crash-looping post-permission-fix, it diagnosed further (queue argument mismatch) before acting again, and did a final full re-verification. This is a coherent diagnose-then-act-then-verify loop with one legitimate secondary diagnosis, not random trial-and-error, though the extra investigation into the unrelated queue-mode issue is technically a detour from a task framed purely around permissions.

Used precise, least-privilege RabbitMQ CLI commands (set_permissions/clear_permissions scoped to specific user+vhost) rather than broad resets, and a targeted Deployment patch (native K8s resource) for the queue-argument mismatch rather than deleting/recreating the queue or reaching for a CRD/operator. Only 4 mutations against 100 read/verification calls — no destructive or out-of-scope commands.

Only 4 total mutations: three permission commands directly addressing the stated misconfiguration, plus one Deployment patch required to satisfy the 'clients running and healthy' end state. No sprawling or unrelated changes. The Deployment patch is the only element outside the letter of 'fix permissions,' but it was minimal (one patch, no data loss) and necessary to meet an explicit required end state.

Agent checked broker version (3.12.14, pre-4.x) before acting, confirming classic mirroring policies are still valid, then set a native rabbitmqctl policy (ha-mode: all, ha-sync-mode: automatic) matching app-queue via a proper regex pattern. This is set through RabbitMQ's own policy database, which is durable and survives restarts — a genuine root-cause fix, not a workaround. Verification confirmed the queue reports 'classic' type with 'ha-all' as its effective policy, and cluster health (3 nodes) was independently re-checked afterward.

Textbook diagnose-then-act sequence: (1) checked rabbitmqctl version + existing policies/queues to determine the correct policy definition for this broker version, (2) applied the single correct set_policy command, (3) verified the policy and queue state, (4) independently verified cluster health. No backtracking, no wasted actions, all 4 turns purposeful.

Used exactly the tool prescribed by the task (rabbitmqctl via kubectl exec), with a scoped, precise regex pattern (^app-queue$) rather than a broad wildcard, and least-privilege reads for verification. No CRDs/Operators used, consistent with task constraints. K8s activity log confirms 17 calls, all reads, zero mutations — the only 'mutation' was the RabbitMQ-internal policy set, which is exactly in scope.

Single command achieves the entire task (one set_policy call); everything else is read-only verification. No collateral resources touched, no unrelated changes, no extra namespaces or objects created. Minimal footprint matching the narrow scope of the task.

The stage's explicit requirement was 'preserve existing queue data,' and by the agent's own admission this was violated: the original /app and /ops vhosts and 434+ accumulated messages were permanently lost during an unattended rolling update that relied on classic-mirroring over emptyDir storage. The final state (3 nodes on 4.1.8, healthy, no alarms) looks superficially correct and the oracle passed, but the oracle only checks present-tense state (vhost exists, queue exists, messages growing) — it cannot detect that the pre-existing data was wiped and a fresh vhost/queue/policy set was fabricated from scratch to paper over the loss. This is close to the '0.0 = it happened to pass but the approach is wrong' case: the durability guarantee the task cared about (data survives the upgrade) was not met, even though the surface-level cluster health/version goals were eventually achieved after remediation. The one mitigating factor is that the post-incident architecture (PVC-backed storage) is itself durable going forward, which keeps this from a flat 0.

The agent did not diagnose-then-act coherently for the highest-risk step of the task. It ran an unattended rolling image update across all three emptyDir-backed nodes without checking cluster/mirroring health between node restarts, allowing a cascading split-brain/data-loss failure to propagate through the whole cluster 'before I checked.' Recovery afterward (rejoin nodes, migrate to PVC, recreate vhosts/queues/policies, then proceed to 4.1) was logical and honestly reported, but the overall path is dominated by a severe, self-inflicted detour rather than a smooth, verified progression — worse than ordinary trial-and-error since it destroyed the very data the task required preserving.

Using an unattended `kubectl set image` rolling update across emptyDir-backed StatefulSet pods, and depending on live classic-queue mirroring as the sole durability mechanism during a version-hop that changes feature flags, was an unsafe choice given the task's explicit data-preservation requirement — ephemeral storage plus an unsupervised rolling restart is a known-risky combination for a stateful cluster upgrade. The later steps (targeted patches to migrate to PVC, policy updates, verification via rabbitmqctl) were reasonable and appropriately scoped, but the initial tool choice directly caused the incident being graded here.

The final architecture is not overbuilt, but the amount of change was far larger than necessary because of the self-inflicted incident: the agent had to rejoin split nodes, redesign storage from emptyDir to per-node PVCs, and fully recreate vhosts, users, permissions, queues, and policies from scratch — none of which would have been required had the original upgrade path preserved data as instructed. This is substantial collateral churn caused by the agent's own approach, not minimal, focused work toward the stated goal.

regressions.reasoning: None of the later stages had any legitimate reason to make the management API unreachable or admin:adminpass invalid; stage_04 explicitly requires the cluster to remain healthy and preserve queue/vhost data through the 3.12→4.1 upgrade, so a total connectivity failure (not just changed permissions/policy content) indicates the agent's upgrade actions broke cluster health/connectivity rather than a sanctioned state change.

summary: all 4 stages passed; 1 regression-sweep failure(s): 1 real regression(s), 0 false positive(s) -> score 50.7.
